artifacts.content:
Mom,

Just sitting here at the commissary between batches of beans and thought I’d send a proper update instead of voice notes.

Lions Bay is looking promising. I’m trying to line up Fridays–Sundays from June 15 through September 15 this summer so the truck has some predictable income. I blocked off August 7–9 though, because I need to be in Surrey Provincial Court with you and Carla for Dad’s sentencing on the impaired driving charge from last year. Not exactly how I pictured spending a summer weekend, but I don’t want him sitting there alone.

On the health side, we’ve got a lot going on. Lucia’s follow‑up cardiology appointment with Dr. Singh at BC Children’s is still July 2. Elena and I are keeping July and early August flexible in case they want to keep her a few days again. Between that and Dad’s court stuff, I feel like I’m planning the schedule around hospitals and judges instead of tides and tourists.

I’m also trying to protect my own brain a bit. My weekly burnout counseling with Dr. Patel at Lions Gate on Tuesdays at 3:00 p.m. has actually been helping. That’s why I’m sticking to weekend service in Lions Bay instead of chasing weekday lunch spots. I’d rather make a bit less and not completely fry myself.

Kids are good. Mateo is still renaming every taco after a dinosaur, and Lucia is into pulling herself up on everything, including Milo’s tail. We’ll try to come out to Surrey one Sunday when we’re not in Lions Bay so Dad can see the kids without it being about lawyers or court.

Love you,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Summer plans, court stuff, and hospital dates

artifacts.content:
Operations / life juggle – running list

• Lions Bay summer window: ideally June 15–Sept 15, Fri–Sun + stat holidays, 11–7. Flag possible no‑show on BC Day (Aug 3, 2026) if Horizon Legal Clinic confirms CRA in‑person repayment meeting that day at the Vancouver office re: 2022–23 tax arrears. Don’t overbook; that GST mess already led to the $450/month plan I set up with CRA back in May 2024.

• Money stress: still chipping away at that older $9,500 CRA payment plan from the 2021 filing error. Remember that when I’m tempted to discount everything. Family Night Combo at $39.95 has to cover food cost + staff + those payments.

• Safety / inspections: follow‑up with West Van Fire before July. During the April 20, 2026 mobile unit inspection (MV‑2026‑184), Mateo had a meltdown in the truck and yanked the loose Class K extinguisher tag. Captain Laura Ng gave me an informal warning at the fire hall and said next time it could be a formal deficiency. Get the tag properly crimped and keep Mateo out of the truck during inspections.

• Generator / electrical: do NOT repeat the March 18, 2026 situation at Dad’s place when the old generator nearly lit up his Surrey driveway and tripped the house breaker while Mom and Dad were babysitting. Rafa’s late‑night rewiring rescue + ESA inspection E‑TRK‑2026‑092 on March 30 saved me. Stick to the approved setup.

• Mental health: keep Tuesdays 3–4 p.m. clear for Dr. Patel at Lions Gate.

• Family: build in at least two full days off in August just for the kids.

metadata.title: Summer 2026 priorities – money, safety, courts, kids

artifacts.content:
Hi Mark,

Just a quick follow‑up now that things have settled down a bit on my end.

Coastal Gas Services came out to the back parking lot on March 18 and completed the annual leak and pressure test on the truck’s propane system. They issued Certificate CGS‑TRK‑2026‑55 confirming everything is up to code. I’ve attached the PDF for your records.

I also wanted to apologize again for the late‑night gas smell incident earlier in March. I was trying to troubleshoot a line after a long service and clearly wasn’t thinking about how it would affect the neighbours. I got your March 10 written warning loud and clear. Going forward I’m not going to do any more propane work or testing at 3175 Mountain Highway at all, day or night. I’ll handle that either at the Harbour View commissary on Marine or at a service yard in Surrey.

On busy weekends I may still park the truck in the back lane from about 5:30 to 8:00 a.m. to do quiet prep—washing cilantro, portioning tortillas, that kind of thing—while my dad drives over from Surrey to watch the kids upstairs. No burners or open flames there, just cold prep so I can get out to Lions Bay on time and still manage childcare with our rent having gone up by $380/month last August.

If any neighbours raise concerns again, please let me know directly and I’ll adjust. We really want to stay on good terms here; this location works for Elena’s commute to Lonsdale and my access to the commissary.

Thanks,
Jordan Reyes
Coastal Cove Tacos & Bowls

metadata.subject: Propane testing update and parking lot use

artifacts.content:
Thread with Elena – saved in notes

Jordan: Hey, just a heads up the kitchen’s going to look like a salsa bomb went off tonight.

Elena: Lol why, what now?

Jordan: I’m trying to get ahead for the first Lions Bay weekend. With Mom’s chemo at Lions Gate since November 2024 and me driving her to those weekday sessions, I’ve been skipping some of my usual Harbour View commissary time. So I’m going to chop veggies and prep salsas here at the apartment kitchen after the kids go down, then load the bins into the truck in the morning.

Elena: As long as you wipe the counters so Lucia doesn’t crawl through onion skins.

Jordan: Promise. It’s just low‑risk stuff—cilantro, onions, limes, portioning tortillas. All the raw fish and chicken still happens in the truck under the hood. On days when you’ve got counselling at North Shore Family Services and I’ve got the kids, I’ve been sneaking over to Harbour View 7–9 p.m. to do the same kind of light prep while you’re in session.

Elena: I know, I appreciate you working around my schedule.

Jordan: Also, I talked to Mom today. Dr. Chandrasekhar at the Fraser Health office in Surrey confirmed that her hepatitis B diagnosis from Surrey Memorial in March doesn’t affect my permit, but she wants me to keep all business prep out of Mom’s condo kitchen while Mom’s in treatment, so I’m keeping it between here, the commissary, and the truck.

Elena: That’s fair. We’ll make our tiny kitchen work. I’ll help label bins after I finish charting.

Jordan: Deal. I’ll bribe you with churro bites testers.

metadata.title: Messages – juggling prep and family health

artifacts.content:
Hey Raf,

Catching up while I wait for rice to finish steaming.

First off, thanks again for bailing me out that night at Dad’s in Surrey when the old generator almost turned his driveway into a campfire and tripped the whole house. I still can’t believe you drove over from Coquitlam at midnight on March 18 to rewire things so we didn’t burn the place down while Mom and Dad were babysitting. The ESA inspection on March 30 (E‑TRK‑2026‑092) signing off on the new setup is basically your handiwork in official form.

I finally bit the bullet and replaced that beast with the quieter Honda EU7000is inverter on April 2 last year after Mrs. Kaur in 304 filed that noise complaint with the strata about me testing in the parkade at 7:15 a.m. It was like $5,200 with the sound box, so I really don’t want to mess this one up.

I’m still a bit worried about noise in places like Lions Bay waterfront. To be honest, even this “quiet” unit isn’t exactly silent, and I don’t have any fancy sound shielding beyond the box and where I park it. I’m kind of counting on the general ambient noise—waves, traffic, kids yelling—to help it blend in for nearby residents.

If you have ideas for further quieting it without cooking it or voiding warranties, let me know. Also, I’m still doing that Pay‑What‑You‑Can bean and rice “Community Bowl” once a month, inspired by those November 2023 evenings outside the North Van Food Bank when I saw Mateo’s preschool classmates’ parents in line. Makes all this extra hassle feel worth it.

Talk soon,
J

metadata.subject: Generator saga + community bowl
metadata.location: Harbour View Commissary Kitchen, North Vancouver

artifacts.content:
Truck layout + neighbourhood impact thoughts – Lions Bay

I keep tweaking the setup depending on where we park. At Harbour View they’re still side‑eyeing us after that March 2024 fire code warning when you, Rafa, left the stockpot of beans unattended on the gas range during that late‑night shift and we had to pay the $450 penalty and do the safety refresher with the manager. Since then I’ve kept all simmering and frying strictly in the truck under the Ansul R‑102 and use the commissary only for cold prep and dishwashing.

That said, when the truck line gets crazy, the built‑in line feels cramped. On those slammed days I usually throw up a folding table beside the truck and plug a couple of countertop burners into whatever outdoor outlet is closest so we can keep tortillas warm or run an extra pot of beans. It’s not glamorous, but it buys us a bit more elbow room.

For quieter residential spots, like what I’m picturing near Lions Bay General Store or Village Hall, I’m also thinking hard about smoke and smell. After Mateo’s asthma flare‑up from all the charcoal smoke at Shipyards Night Market and the July 22, 2023 ER visit at BC Children’s, I ditched open‑flame grilling and spent about $3,800 upgrading the exhaust hood and filters at the commissary in October 2023. I’ve got us on a four‑week professional hood‑cleaning schedule now so we’re not blasting neighbours with grease smoke.

Reminder: keep queue parallel to the truck, do the 5‑minute litter sweep every hour, and photo the site at close, especially in small communities like Lions Bay.

Event: Weekly counseling – burnout / anxiety
Date: Every Tuesday, recurring – 2026-01-06 to 2026-12-22
Time: 3:00 p.m. – 4:00 p.m.
Location: Lions Gate Hospital, Outpatient Mental Health (4th floor)
Attendees: Jordan Reyes; Dr. Patel

Notes:
- Leave commissary or truck by 2:20 p.m. to make parking at LGH.
- This is non‑negotiable time off the truck. No Lions Bay shifts on Tuesdays, even in peak season (June 15–Sept 15, 2026).
- Use sessions to keep burnout in check so I don’t repeat 2021, when I was in the Lions Gate outpatient program cooking Mom’s adobo‑style pulled chicken taco every Thursday as a grounding exercise.
- Bring up: juggling Lucia’s follow‑up cardiology appointment with Dr. Singh at BC Children’s on July 2, Dad’s impaired driving sentencing in Surrey Provincial Court Aug 7–9, and the CRA arrears meeting that might land on BC Day (Aug 3).
- Also talk through the guilt around Mom’s health: her early‑stage breast cancer diagnosis at Lions Gate in Nov 2024, the hepatitis B diagnosis from Surrey Memorial, and her being immunocompromised on biologics at Surrey Memorial’s autoimmune clinic since late 2022. This is why I’m still strict about COVID protocols on the truck (no sick staff, masks during surges, 30–45 min surface sanitizing when we’re near places like Lions Bay General Store and Village Hall parking lot).

Reminder: block out 2:30–4:30 p.m. in calendar so I’m not tempted to squeeze in commissary prep before or after.

From: Jordan Alexander Reyes <jordan.reyes@coastalcovetacos.com>
To: Ana Lourdes Reyes <[redacted]>
Date: 2026-04-11 21:18
Subject: New menu ideas based on Dad’s diet

Hi Mom,

I’ve been playing around with some new menu ideas for the truck and wanted your input, especially about Dad.

I’m seriously thinking of adding a low-sodium, GERD‑friendly chicken and rice bowl to the Coastal Cove lineup this summer. It’s basically the same one I’ve been making for Dad since his hypertension scare at Surrey Memorial Hospital back in March 2023 – poached chicken, no skin, no deep fry, low-salt garlic-ginger broth, steamed rice instead of cilantro-lime, and grilled zucchini instead of the pickled onions that set off his reflux. Customers keep asking for “lighter” options, and this actually tastes good without blowing up anyone’s blood pressure.

I’m also revisiting that dairy‑free mango agua fresca recipe I was testing in July 2024 in the family kitchen at #204 – 3175 Mountain Highway, when Elena was dealing with postpartum reflux after childbirth with Lucia. Remember how we kept tweaking it so she could tolerate it – no dairy, less citrus, more water, a bit of coconut sugar instead of the regular syrup? I think it could work as a summer special if I keep it gentle enough for sensitive stomachs.

Can you ask Dad how he’s been feeling with the latest version of the bowl and if there’s anything that still bothers his GERD? I want to be able to honestly say it’s “Miguel-approved.”

Love,
Jordan

metadata.subject: New menu ideas based on Dad’s diet

artifacts.content:
Title: Menu dev notes – “Miguel bowl” + “Elena mango fresca”
Date: 2026-04-13
Author: Jordan A. Reyes
Location: Harbour View Commissary Kitchen, North Vancouver

– Goal: finalize list of food and beverages to be sold for summer 2026 events (Lions Bay weekends + Sea-to-Sky pop-ups).

1) “Miguel Bowl” – low-sodium, GERD‑friendly chicken & rice
   • Origin: adapted from Dad’s post–hypertension scare diet after Surrey Memorial Hospital stay (March 2023).
   • Components: poached chicken breast (no skin), low‑sodium garlic‑ginger broth, plain steamed rice, grilled zucchini + carrots, fresh cilantro only on side, no pickled onions, no raw jalapeño.
   • Seasoning: minimal salt; use lime zest instead of juice to keep it more GERD‑friendly.
   • Marketing angle: “Inspired by Miguel Reyes – father, heart‑healthy and reflux‑conscious.”

2) “Elena Mango Fresca” – dairy‑free mango agua fresca
   • Origin: version I developed in July 2024 in the family kitchen at #204 – 3175 Mountain Highway, North Vancouver, so Elena Marie Santos could drink it during her postpartum reflux after childbirth with Lucia Amara Reyes.
   • Components: ripe mango purée, filtered water, tiny bit of coconut sugar, pinch of salt; absolutely no dairy, no added citrus.
   • Notes: emphasize “dairy‑free, low‑acid, refreshing.”

3) Integration with existing menu
   • Add both items as rotating specials alongside standard tacos, bowls, churro bites, lime coconut rice pudding, and non‑alcoholic beverages.
   • Test at two weekend services; track sales + feedback, especially from guests with GERD/hypertension concerns.

metadata.title: Menu dev notes – “Miguel bowl” + “Elena mango fresca”
metadata.location: Harbour View Commissary Kitchen, North Vancouver
